methodsAttempted.query: site:qub.ac.uk Queen's University Belfast generative AI policy students assessment
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search mostly returned 402 credit errors; one Firecrawl search found the official responsible AI in research page. General web search found Queen's AI Hub, assessment guidance, and research-integrity pages.
Identified official Queen's DigiHub AI pages for position, tools, responsible use, student support, assessment, and research.

methodsAttempted.query: AI Hub internal links and linked PDFs/login-gated pages
methodsAttempted.notes: Followed internal AI Hub links and classified non-public SharePoint/HR links and non-QUB external sector PDFs as rejected.

artifacts.summary: Official-source discovery found Queen's DigiHub AI Hub pages covering QUB AI position, tools, responsible use, assessment, student support, and responsible AI in research, plus a concise central Research Integrity AI page on qub.ac.uk. Research publications, course marketing pages, external sector PDFs, and login-gated SharePoint/HR resources were not used as evidence.

artifacts.claimText: Queen's AI position page says its staff and student AI guidance is based on RAISE principles: responsible use, AI best practice, integrity, support, and equitable access.
artifacts.normalizedValue: RAISE principles frame staff and student AI guidance

artifacts.sourceTitle: Understanding QUB's Position on AI - RAISE Principles - Queen's DigiHub
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal:
This section brings together tailored QUB guidance for staff and students. This includes recommendations on how to get started as well as more specific guidance on the use of AI, for example within assessment.
Our guidance is based on the following principles:
R
esponsible use,
A
I best practice,
I
ntegrity,
S
upport and
E
quitable Access – collectively RAISE.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: QUB describes its staff and student AI guidance as based on Responsible use, AI best practice, Integrity, Support and Equitable Access.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Responsible Use of AI in Education: Ensuring Ethical AI - Queen's DigiHub
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: When using an AI tool, it is necessary to make an ethical judgment about the data or information that you put into the system when you use it to complete a task. Any information that is submitted to an AI tool then becomes part of the data that the tool draws upon to complete future tasks for anyone who uses the tool. You need to consider whether you have permission to submit the information that you do.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Users are told to judge whether they have permission before submitting information to AI tools.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Responsible Use of AI > Privacy and Security

artifacts.claimText: Queen's assessment guidance says text-based AI detectors are not recommended because current tools cannot definitively identify AI-authored content and can produce false positives.

citation.citationTitle: AI in Assessment and Feedback: Opportunities and Challenges - Queen's DigiHub
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI in Assessment and Feedback: Opportunities and Challenges - Queen's DigiHub
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal:
Current tools that attempt to detect AI generated text – whether by analysing writing styles, using machine learning classification, or watermarking – cannot definitively identify AI-authored content. Worryingly, these systems often produce an unacceptably high rate of false positives.
In the future, with the integration of AI writing tools into platforms like Microsoft Word and Google Workplace, it is anticipated that much of our writing will include AI-generated elements. This will be similar to how we currently benefit from algorithm-driven spell checkers and grammar tools. Considering these factors, the use of text-based AI detectors is not recommended.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The assessment page says current AI text detectors cannot definitively identify AI-authored content and are not recommended.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal:
This guidance applies to:
All Queen’s researchers (staff, postgraduate research students, visiting researchers, and contractors) conducting research under the auspices of the University.
All types of research activities across the research lifecycle including planning, funding proposal development, data collection and analysis, publication and dissemination, peer review and evaluation, and research management.

artifacts.title: Queen's University Belfast staged AI policy source report
artifacts.summary: Staged official-source bundle for Queen's University Belfast. Public Queen's DigiHub and Research Integrity pages support narrow candidates on RAISE-framed AI guidance, Copilot access, public-data caution for tool exploration, ethical data submission, assessment clarification, academic misconduct treatment, AI detector caution, student AI resources, and responsible AI use in research. Course pages, research publication records, external sector PDFs, and login-gated SharePoint/HR links were rejected or not used as evidence.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
Claims are staged candidates only and are not marked canonical or human reviewed.
Login-gated SharePoint and HR training resources were not accessed or used as evidence.
External Russell Group and Jisc resources were treated as context only, not Queen's University Belfast evidence.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
